q: When must wages be paid to employees?
expected: Under Article 103, wages shall be paid at least once every two weeks or twice a month at intervals not exceeding sixteen days. No employer shall pay wages less frequently than once a month.

q: What forms of payment are prohibited for wages?
expected: Under Article 102, no employer shall pay wages by means of promissory notes, vouchers, coupons, tokens, tickets, chits, or any object other than legal tender. Wages must be paid in cash.

q: Where must wages be paid to employees?
expected: Under Article 104, payment of wages shall be made at or near the place of undertaking, except as otherwise provided by regulations of the Secretary of Labor.

q: Can wages be paid to someone other than the employee?
expected: Under Article 105, wages shall be paid directly to the workers. Exceptions include force majeure, where payment may be made through another person with written authority from the worker, or in case of death, where wages may be paid to the heirs without intestate proceedings.

q: What happens to workers' wages if the employer goes bankrupt?
expected: Under Article 110, in the event of bankruptcy or liquidation of an employer's business, workers shall enjoy first preference as regards their wages and monetary claims. Such unpaid wages shall be paid in full before the claims of the government and other creditors.

q: What deductions are allowed from an employee's wages?
expected: Under Article 113, no employer shall make deductions from wages except: when the worker is insured with his consent and the deduction recompenses the employer for insurance premiums paid; when the worker requests deduction for union dues; or when deduction is authorized by law or regulations.

q: Can an employer withhold wages or demand kickbacks from workers?
expected: Under Article 116, it is unlawful for any person to withhold any amount from the wages of a worker or induce him to give up any part of his wages by force, stealth, intimidation, threat, or any other means without the worker's consent.

q: Can an employer reduce wages of an employee who filed a complaint?
expected: Under Article 118, it is unlawful for an employer to refuse to pay or reduce wages and benefits, discharge, or discriminate against any employee who has filed a complaint or testified in proceedings under the Labor Code.

q: Who sets the minimum wage in each region of the Philippines?
expected: Under Article 99, minimum wage rates for agricultural and non-agricultural employees in each region shall be prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards.

q: What factors are considered in fixing the regional minimum wage?
expected: Under Article 124, in determining regional minimum wages, the Regional Board considers factors necessary to maintain minimum standards of living for health, efficiency, and general well-being of employees, within the framework of national economic and social development programs.

q: Can workers bargain for wages higher than the minimum wage order?
expected: Under Article 125, no wage order shall be construed to prevent workers in particular firms or enterprises from bargaining for higher wages with their respective employers.

q: Can a wage order issued by a regional board lower existing wages?
expected: Under Article 127, no wage order issued by any regional board shall provide for wage rates lower than the statutory minimum wage rates prescribed by Congress.

q: Who is liable for wages if a contractor fails to pay its workers?
expected: Under Article 106, if a contractor or subcontractor fails to pay the wages of employees, the principal employer shall be jointly and severally liable with the contractor to such employees to the extent of the work performed under the contract.

q: What is the maximum duration of an apprenticeship agreement?
expected: Under Article 61, the period of apprenticeship shall not exceed six months. Apprenticeship agreements may provide for wage rates below the legal minimum wage but not lower than 75 percent of the applicable minimum wage.

q: Who may employ apprentices under the Labor Code?
expected: Under Article 60, only employers in highly technical industries may employ apprentices, and only in apprenticeable occupations approved by the Minister of Labor and Employment.

q: What is a learner under the Labor Code?
expected: Under Article 73, learners are persons hired as trainees in semi-skilled and other industrial occupations which are non-apprenticeable and which may be learned through practical training on the job in a relatively short period of time not exceeding three months.

q: What must a learnership agreement contain?
expected: Under Article 75, a learnership agreement must include: the names and addresses of learners; the duration of the learnership period which shall not exceed three months; the wage rates which shall not be less than 75 percent of the applicable minimum wage; and a commitment to employ the learners as regular employees upon completion if they so desire.

q: Can an employer require a female employee to stay unmarried as a condition of employment?
expected: Under Article 134, it is unlawful for an employer to require as a condition of employment or continuation of employment that a woman employee shall not get married, or to stipulate that upon getting married she shall be deemed resigned or separated.

q: Are women working in night clubs or massage clinics considered employees?
expected: Under Article 136, any woman who is permitted or suffered to work with or without compensation in any night club, cocktail lounge, massage clinic, bar or similar establishment under the effective control of the employer for a substantial period of time shall be considered an employee for purposes of labor and social legislation.

q: What protections are given to women night workers during and after pregnancy?
expected: Under Article 158, measures shall be taken to ensure that an alternative to night work is available to women workers before and after childbirth for a period of at least sixteen weeks. Additional periods may be granted based on a medical certificate stating it is necessary for the health of the mother or child.

q: Who is covered by the domestic service chapter of the Labor Code?
expected: Under Article 139, the domestic service chapter applies to all persons rendering services in households for compensation. Domestic or household service includes service necessary for the maintenance and enjoyment of the employer's home, including services of family drivers.

q: How much notice is required to end a domestic service arrangement?
expected: Under Article 148, if the duration of household service is not determined either by stipulation or by the nature of the service, the employer or the househelper may give notice to end the relationship five days before the intended termination.

q: Is a domestic worker entitled to an employment certificate upon separation?
expected: Under Article 149, upon the severance of the household service relation, the employer shall give the househelper a written statement of the nature and duration of the service and his or her efficiency and conduct as househelper.

q: When can a private employment agency charge a fee to a worker?
expected: Under Article 32, a private fee-charging employment agency shall not charge any fee until the worker has obtained employment through its efforts or has actually commenced employment. Such fee must always be covered by an appropriate receipt.

q: Can an alien worker change employers after being issued an employment permit?
expected: Under Article 41, after the issuance of an employment permit, the alien shall not transfer to another job or change his employer without prior approval of the Secretary of Labor. Violation may result in deportation after service of sentence.

q: Who is responsible for setting occupational safety and health standards?
expected: Under Article 168, the Secretary of Labor and Employment shall set and enforce mandatory occupational safety and health standards to eliminate or reduce hazards in all workplaces and ensure safe and healthful working conditions.

q: Is an employer required to provide first-aid treatment in the workplace?
expected: Under Article 162, every employer shall keep in his establishment first-aid medicines and equipment as the nature and conditions of work may require. The employer shall also take steps for the training of a sufficient number of employees in first-aid treatment.

q: What is the purpose of the employees' compensation program?
expected: Under Article 172, the State promotes and develops a tax-exempt employees' compensation program whereby employees and their dependents, in the event of work-connected disability or death, may promptly secure adequate income benefits and medical-related benefits.

q: Who is considered an employer of homeworkers under the Labor Code?
expected: Under Article 153, the employer of homeworkers includes any person who, for his own account or on behalf of any person, directly or indirectly delivers goods or materials to be processed in a home and thereafter to be returned or disposed of in accordance with his directions.

q: Who is covered under the night worker provisions of the Labor Code?
expected: Under Article 154, the night worker provisions apply to all persons employed or permitted to work at night — except those in agriculture, stock raising, fishing, maritime transport, and inland navigation — during a period of not less than seven consecutive hours including the interval from midnight to five in the morning.
